question: A 60-year-old woman underwent endoscopic transluminal drainage with a lumen‑apposing metal stent and subsequently required surgical repair of a procedure‑related gastric wall injury. Endoscopic closure attempts have since failed. Based on the imaging, which of the following safety considerations is most important before attempting another endoscopic closure?

options.A: Large defect / immature cavity wall precluding durable closure
options.B: Active systemic infection requiring IV antibiotics

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT image for features relevant to endoscopic closure of a gastric wall injury. I see a large, low-attenuation fluid collection occupying the left upper abdomen/posterior gastric region that closely abuts the posterior wall of the stomach. The posterior gastric wall appears irregular with a focal discontinuity/indentation where the collection contacts the stomach, consistent with a sizable defect or cavity in continuity with the gastric wall.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical question, which asks what safety consideration is most important before attempting another endoscopic closure. A successful endoscopic closure requires a relatively small defect and a mature, well-formed cavity wall to hold sutures or clips; the image instead shows a large collection and an apparent wide defect, suggesting an immature cavity margin and a high risk of closure failure.

Then I consider the other options in light of the image. There is no clear CT evidence of active infected gas locules or a rim-enhancing abscess on this slice to indicate uncontrolled local infection (option B is not supported by this image). There is no gross hematoma or imaging sign of bleeding/coagulopathy (option C cannot be assessed from the CT and is not suggested by the image). I also do not see a high‑density metallic stent traversing the wall in this image, so a retained transmural stent is not apparent (option D is not supported).

Finally, synthesizing these observations: the dominant and directly visible safety concern on imaging is the large defect with an immature/unstable cavity wall that would likely preclude a durable endoscopic closure at this time. Therefore option A best matches the imaging findings.
caption: Symptomatic pseudocyst.
image_context: A 60-year-old woman presented with acute pancreatitis complicated by the development of acute pancreatic fluid collection, which later organized into a large peripancreatic pseudocyst (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>). This was drained endoscopically with a lumen-apposing metal stent inserted under EUS guidance (). This was drained endoscopically with a lumen-apposing metal stent inserted under EUS guidance (<xref rid="gr2" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2</xref>). The patient experienced a gastric perforation after the procedure, possibly resulting from an immature cyst wall, which required surgical repair (). The patient experienced a gastric perforation after the procedure, possibly resulting from an immature cyst wall, which required surgical repair (<xref rid="gr3" ref-type="fig">Fig. 3</xref>). Two months later, she had a residual gastric fistula along the posterior gastric wall communicating with the pancreatic bed (). Two months later, she had a residual gastric fistula along the posterior gastric wall communicating with the pancreatic bed (<xref rid="gr4" ref-type="fig">Figure 4</xref>, , <xref rid="gr5" ref-type="fig">Figure 5</xref>, , <xref rid="gr6" ref-type="fig">Figure 6</xref>). The opening was too big to be closed with over-the-scope clips. We performed endoscopic suturing, which failed to close the fistula despite the achievement of good mucosal approximation.). The opening was too big to be closed with over-the-scope clips. We performed endoscopic suturing, which failed to close the fistula despite the achievement of good mucosal approximation.Figure 1Symptomatic pseudocyst.Figure 2EUS-guided lumen-apposing metal stent.Figure 3CT showing postcystogastrostomy gastric perforation.Figure 4Fistula in the posterior gastric wall communicating with the pancreatic bed.Figure 5Gastric fistula.Figure 6Upper-GI study showing leak.
relevant_image_context: A 60-year-old woman presented with acute pancreatitis complicated by the development of acute pancreatic fluid collection, which later organized into a large peripancreatic pseudocyst (Fig. 1). Figure 1 Symptomatic pseudocyst.
